video_title: Sui Co-Founder: No Funds Were Lost In The Outage - Here's Why
transcript: As you realize there was no money [music] lost in this situation and this is by design. Obviously the ideal option is to halt compared to just losing money if something else happens right there is obviously war room calling everybody across the world because you know we're a remote first company there were people across the world working I was in uh Europe people were in the United States everybody came to the same room I think we did the best thing to in decentralized world because it's not one company pressing a button right but unlike Facebook because you know some of us are coming from Facebook [music] and back then the motto was uh you know move fast break things I don't [music] believe on this situation if it was in another blockchain that issue without having these brains behind it I don't believe they would recover even even in >> welcome Costas welcome here at proof of talk um you're the first one today >> thank you so much >> um we need to talk about everything that has been happening when it comes to sooie and sometimes some good things happen sometimes some challenging periods are taking place and I think the last part can definitely be the case right now with the events of the past few weeks. Can you tell us and the audience what has happened with the aishes or the errors that took place within the SUI network? >> Of course. Of course. As everybody knows, SU is one of the most innovative blockchains out there. Uh potentially the fastest one and obviously we try to build some of the new cool features that um the modern blockchain should have. In this situation, we have um an incident where trying to offer some new functionality on SUI which was related to address balances. So we have uh specific balances on on your account for all of your tokens. There was an issue with the gas massing and this code uh like um an issue on the validator side uh which created some outads uh for a few hours. Um obviously when you're innovating so fast um it is possible sometimes that you might miss something that is not humanly possible especially with um uh like the innovation rate we're having but unlike Facebook because you know some of us are coming from Facebook and back then the moto was uh you know move fast break things I don't believe on this situation it was uh literally an outcome of this because the team was focusing on this future for more than six months and imagine these are people used to work at Google Facebook Apple uh Netflix obviously there was a net case that we didn't cut and this unfortunately created a cascading effect where solving Quan then uh we we created a low probability situation that it can cause again uh a similar issue and then eventually we found uh like the solution that uh forever will solve this uh this particular problem but I can assure you that um the team and the brains working under this uh particular feature and um you've seen how Evan the CEO of Mistlabs and the head of uh like this particular uh offering uh uh Mark they were very um uh like accurate on look six months of some of the biggest brains um we're humans there might be mistakes but at the same time I think the effort that is paid now to make the the system by far more robust than than ever is probably one of the best lessons we had okay these things are happening but at the same time I believe the uh if it was in another blockchain that issue without having these brains behind it, I don't believe they would recover even even in in in days, right? So I feel I feel at least for my team uh blessed um in the sense that when you're trying to pretty much go very close to the speed of light, you want things to uh to happen properly. We're learning from this. Of course, uh this is a lesson for us as well, but I believe we're in a good direction here um to uh to be able to keep this robustness and the fast innovation that we want. I think a mistake is not bad that when it happens like an edge case can happen. I run a fund and there's sometimes some stuff that is taking place that we couldn't oversight or couldn't see beforehand. But one of the critical factors that do happen during these events is that there is a big gap between what the audience audience knows and what you guys know. So how are you going to essentially bridge the trust or like rebuild the trust again? So the audience and the community says well we can trust you again >> 100%. Uh by the way as you realize there was no money lost in this situation and this is by design in the first occasion where there was an issue at the consensus layer for uh for this particular case. Obviously the ideal option is to hold compared to just losing money if something else happens. Right? So this one didn't affect any any balances any any assets on every uh like on any client. And in my opinion, this was a big win in the sense that the system at least um had this like you know backup uh mode where if something wrong happens at least protect all of the balances it worked. >> So in this occasion imagine what's happening when there is an incident. There is obviously a war room calling everybody across the world because you know we're remote first company. There were people across the world working I was in uh Europe people were in the United States. Everybody came to the same room uh more than 30 50 people actually eventually working on the same uh uh like goal. Let's solve the problem and let's figure out how we can uh bring the network back. Obviously at this stage what's happening is you're running whatever possible from the security team. you need to make all of the analysis whatever you are proposing as a fix is a correct thing that doesn't break something else that loses money first and in this situation uh like some of the biggest brains in u in the organization along with external uh people who helped us on uh on this scenario and this is always happening I'm also an auditor and someone who is participating in other wars in other blockchains I think we did the best thing to in decentralized world because it's not one company pressing a button right and you are up in the next moment You have to go to the validators. You have to tell them, look guys, do we have a fix? It takes some time for all of them to go up and running. Some of them are not in time zones that are like awake and imagine all of them have to know properly to be educated what they are voting for, what they're bringing back to to life. And in my opinion, the the time we spent on uh this coordination was super paramount to to go up and protect the balances at the same time and fixing the problem after uh like a couple of iterations. Um in my opinion this was um this is pretty much what's happening in every blockchain right I was as I told you auditor in in multiple places before I even joined miston um this is natural what's happening in in the background you cannot imagine there is stress of course at that time but you cannot just press the button and uh fix something without going through thorough tests right and this is why you see sometimes a few things taking uh more than a few minutes >> yeah so I know that you have been talking about this on a different podcast, but your sleep schedule is quite uh diverse from someone else in the sense that you're not making full nights and you're writing stuff everywhere. >> How has it been during these weeks? [laughter] >> Look, [snorts] my average sleeping time is 5 hours at most. >> Okay. >> Um unfortunately, this is pretty much um almost a habit now. I've learned this uh you know this trick from my professor in the university the mathematician one then the cryptographer where you're sleeping in power naps and then you have a few hours you are very proactive uh like and very uh competitive then go back to sleep again that's why in all of my offices I typically have a tent or a place where I'm taking a quick nap these days it was um especially because it happened uh you know just before the weekend >> and it was a long weekend in uh in a few uh like countries including Greece where I am. Um, so I had of course to take some time from my sleep again [laughter] and uh sleep next day. But look, uh, by the time I've seen the latest fix and everybody was happy with that, I think I had a good sleep at least for I don't know, seven hours to cover all of the previous days that I didn't sleep properly. >> Well, I think in terms of of timing, it was also we just uh I've been to the event in Miami where a lot of updates were uh pitched by Adon. Maybe it's good to focus on that again like what is Sue trying to build or what are the latest updates that are as big as they are within the SUI network. >> 100%. So in my opinion one of the best features that we are offering now is that payments for stable coins are for free. >> Mhm. >> Um you cannot imagine we had this discussion even with my wife and a few friends who are not digents. These people are regular traders or not even traders, right? They want to go into the blockchain space. They hear about crypto but they I mean the average person cannot see imagine if you are an Instagram user you cannot see 0.001 face right >> so we try to remove this um uh pretty much bottleneck for uh getting new people into the space by offering uh free transfers on stable coins. >> Um in my opinion this is one of the biggest wins uh in the space because you get at least in par with what the new modern banking is happening. Imagine in the past in some countries you still have to pay to transfer money from one bank to the other even in the same continent. Right? So um this is probably one of the best features we offered and we gradually see with all of the innovations we have where people can join into the crypto space by just logging in with a Facebook account or with Google account. No need to remember long passwords and all of these things actually makes it easier for the first time to use blockchain for payments. Mhm. >> Otherwise it was just um you know an asset that you keep forever there and is it a coin or is it a stock right? So in our situation I think we broke this um this particular perception uh that crypto is only uh like for for trading and also it helps the agents because they're coming right we have the AI agents >> they don't want to deal with um uh with all of this you know small gas fees and so on plus there is another benefit of this where some banking systems where they don't want uh like by regulation to use crypto like the actual crypto they can still use just stable coins to do their transactions on the crypto space without paying us and you can you can imagine how how difficult is it on the accounting when you have to explain oh I paid this amount of sui but I wanted to transfer uh dollars. So in this in this occasion I believe we did a a very good job into porting into the system something that is for the average user who wants to be on boarded here. So this is definitely one of the best things. The other thing is so preparing itself for the agentic economy and I think um even from what happened uh last week we're having one of the most in my opinion sophisticated AI systems for back detection and at the same time filtering of the transaction and also giving opening the door for AI agents to transact if it's free they can do it even as experiments right so we we encourage like people who are building on AI to transact on sui at the moment >> I think there are a lot great factors there and I think there are some other updates as well within the SUI ecosystem but if someone is doesn't know Sooie um it can be a little bit a mess in terms of that there are so many projects being built on on top of SUI like the ecosystem what is the elevator pitch that you tell to someone that is unaware of what Sooie is doing and wants to understand more >> I believe one of the biggest factors that I can see startups actually building consu is um this innovation rate the features that we have. Imagine if you're building something like a bank right and and you want your clients to have free transactions obviously the obvious case if you want to go into the crypto space is sui if you are someone who wants to use like privacy preserving techniques for example there is zero knowledge proofs enabled on sui at the moment we even have uh like authenticators where you don't even like even google doesn't know you're logging in with google but google doesn't know it's you that you have an account in sui we do have a few um uh like primitive that do not exist anywhere else. Uh for example, you know, we have a parallel execution with these objects >> and the object is a natural way for anything AI to understand ownership, to understand what it means. I mean, it's it's like the casual programming languages we had in the past. I believe what was um not a mistake but uh definitely a decision from the older blockchains like I don't know Ethereum or Salana that they didn't have objects in in the way that we do is they try to compress everything to make the performance fast for their blockchain. What we did is we added parallelization bringing back the objects and this obviously makes whatever AI is uh trained against I don't know some other languages JavaScript, Rust, Java that is now more capable of doing uh blockchain operations with SUI. So I can expect many people who are doing automated trading or even RWAS for which you want a certificate, you want an object, right? You want something that is tangible. SU is probably the best case to to go there along with a few things that I personally see coming here that we're preparing ourselves for the quantum world and at the same time we are we're building uh like an infrastructure for the memory of the AI agents how do you do big operations and like heavy data on the blockchain you cannot do it now the nice thing with sui is we created on the side um like the team behind uh uh like mist labs walrus as well and this one created an ecosystem where by default it's creating an umbrella for AI operations very fast operations on sui memory portable memory on walrus and then you have all of the other things around it right so it's it's natural in my opinion to have one L1 all of the liquidity is there it's not split between the L2s and everything and then you can have a gamer coming into your trading platform and so on otherwise we're in silos and I'm telling you this story because you know I'm I'm originally Greek right and we have the islands go live in a Greek island on the winter you cannot it's just nobody's there. >> So it it seems that we need a place where everything is happening at the same time and the protocols can exchange users naturally. So in my opinion this is the biggest element of sweep super fast as an L1 with many primitives even on AI u ait uh uh features where people can can build can build stuff and you will see the innovation rate especially now that we're building like a very robust secure mechanisms here is going to to continue like it is at the moment before we continue with this episode I would like to thank my sponsors and one of them is OKX. OKX is the place where I'm trading futures and doing day trades. And the reason that I'm using OKX is because it's a final month before the Mica license is going to be in play, which means that if your exchange doesn't have the Mica license, it can actually cancel everything of your trades and ban you completely in your country. And that's why it's super important to use an exchange that is Mica compliant. And that's why I moved everything into OKX as it has three licenses already and they are running a deposit match which means that you get a bonus up to 5% on your deposit if you go to OKX. Make sure to use our link and start trading on OKX. Now let's get back to the episode. How do you see um in terms of like the growth that you are having but how do you see the importance of being decentralized with decentralized compute versus having centralized parties? >> Many many reasons for this. Um imagine that um again I will use the uh the Greek uh like nationality that I have. We had capital controls in Greece >> like a few years ago. This situation can also happen at the same time on anything that is operating in centralized services. you know there is geopolitical situations um do you trust this country the other country and so on we need to go in a decentralized way and it's not only that in my opinion the finance itself when you have to go around different regulations when you have to go to GDPR in the European Union in different restrictions somewhere else the decentralized system is offering uh pretty much a door to be able to have some compatibility between uh various regions [snorts] so I'm a big believer of decentralization Um I'm I'm in the Bitcoin uh since since ever like literally the very first year. Um this is when I met my wife as well in a in a Bitcoin conference. So I'm I'm personally one of the biggest fans of this and I cannot see especially now with AI and with our data moving around like centralized companies how you can feel comfortable and what will happen in your data in the next two years. Right? at least decentralization. You have this, you know, transparency and the ownership that in my opinion was missing by the the generalized financial system or anything that had to do with our data, even social networks. I'm coming from Facebook, right? I used to work for uh for Mark and I was uh on the Libra project as a lead cryptographer there. Um at the same time working on WhatsApp as well. So I've seen what is it is to be in a social network. This is this is an opportunity now to create at least an infrastructure that gives the the users the power to know and even monetize from their data, >> right? I I should be I should earn some money in order for you to send me a spammy email. >> I should Yes. But because this this is reality, right? And it's not happening today. And the same thing is by being able to run my automated bots without having any any restrictions originally until there is a reason like it's a restriction reason. But you can do it in other jurisdictions, in other places. So I think it opens the gate for experimentation and new ideas for the startup. I cannot see how this can happen in a centralized world. >> I think it's a good um comparison in the sense that I'm a content creator myself and the amount of money that I receive from platforms like YouTube or X is like nothing compared to the amount of impressions that I generate. So most of the revenue goes towards those platforms. But when we talk about like full decentralization, um you come from Libra. Yeah. >> Um it essentially got killed down by the government. How do we in some way? Yeah. But how do we uh [laughter] >> there are many reasons for this. >> Yeah. Yeah. I'm just phrasing it very shortly, but uh how do we prevent that governments are going to step in and take back control over decentralization and over the platforms that we're currently building that are potentially making the world a little bit better? 100%. This is a great question and actually I think we have a few um both opinions but also solutions here. >> I like both opinions. >> One one of the things that decentralization is actually offering now is this cryptographic agility. The fact that you own your assets and nobody can come and create a capital control in your case is actually one of the monumental uh in my opinion features that decentralization brought to humanity. Right. Again no capital controls here. >> That's the reality. they cannot even I mean even if there is there is something and um like let's assume that the government doesn't want this you can go and transact in another place using your same balance your same identity uh then there is privacy right because we're talking about the the asset you know robustness and the fact that you own it at the same time how do you know even as a content creator they tell you that these are the the number of views in your u in your video how do you know >> yeah I mean >> you trust them right >> true >> you trust And there were a few situations even on X and Twitter where you don't see necessarily who liked your content and so on only maybe the the owner can see and then you have to accept that it happened. But the centralization is offering this transparency element that even if a government tries to do something at least we will know what they they they did on on the background. And in my opinion this is super important. There is liability in decentralization. there is if someone tries to make a I don't know an attack into a system for example let's assume in bitcoin they go and get all of the mining uh devices and so on eventually we will realize because we will see the transfer of devices from China to the United States to Europe to Russia and in in in different places and when you know especially in our system that is a voting based validator system you see exactly what its validator is doing right it's not so and in my opinion this is a better system compared to proof of work because here it's even more transparent. proof of work at least you can run in some hydro electric power plants uh some uh grids and so on. Nobody knows right. Eventually we will learn by the transfer of hardware. But at least here with voting you can see okay these entities are voting. I mean you can go and check who are these entities. Do they have any collaboration between them? Is it the same entity? Because I see sometimes decentralization in the grand scheme of uh like events here. Um we have I don't know some other protocols that they they do have multiple validators but it's the same entity eventually behind it. At least here you can see who is voting and for what purposes are voting. And now with the crypto uh agility features you can add whatever programmability you want. You don't like something that the uh like some government is doing. Okay, create a smart contract that in this situation you are more protected against these activities. >> So this is how how things are evolving in a more transparent open-source way. I cannot see how this can happen either with centralization or with anything that is living in one single country. Yeah, I think there are two roots when it comes to that because you mentioned the example of social media where a lot of content creators within crypto have been gamifying their fuse in order to get a higher revenue out of protocols that they were they were pushing on their socials. If you want to stop that, the first route is doing it through full KYC in order to like have actual honest views. The other way around is probably CK proofs, I guess, >> 100%. So, How can we how can we build that when it comes to government roles and also you protecting your own privacy and interacting with other institutions >> 100%. So for the people who don't know what zero knowledge proof is right it's a privacy preserving mechanism. So technically you can prove you did something without showing exactly what it is. The easiest example for identity is to understand that I can prove if I put my passport into a zero knowledge proof that I'm over 18 or 21 and I can go and buy some things that I'm not allowed uh if I'm not an adult, right? Um these techniques actually exist and SUI has an even uh like native function to be able to do zero knowledge proofs. I mean even now we're building confidential transactions here where people can transfer balances and nobody else knows apart from the uh from the users and the issuer maybe if they want to enforce some regulation what's how how much money is transferred from whom and in my opinion this is going to be one of the holy grails the most important innovation in the cryptography space in the last few years is zero knowledge Bruce and we're [snorts] going to see it because the blockchain engineers and cryptographers are by far more superior than people who are working even for agencies. You can imagine why, right? People here are like working on very cool stuff. They are um they're uh like paid well in the sense that they are doing the things that they want. They they publish things. They are doing whatever they could imagine not being just academics and that's why you see on the blockchain space being the place of most innovations happening in cryptography in the last 15 years. Yeah. So in in that sense I cannot imagine another place in the world to be able to sustain this zero knowledge proof authenticators zero knowledge proof identity um uh apart from the blockchain this is the only way you can you can do it at least this will be the start 100% sure web two will follow but by our paradigm and um I'm strongly believer on this go and check all of the published papers on cryptography most of them are from blockchain companies and we're super happy to have this like you know badge Like even now we have more than 100 white papers being published as sui miston walrus just because we have one of the strongest uh teams in the world. >> Mhm. If we look at the entire ecosystem then there's the constant debate of sooie versus salana or sooie versus apptos or any of the other um chains out there. Um how relevant do you see the entire debate when it comes to we can have everything onto one chain or we can have multiple chains just integrating each other? >> Look um the the one chain is solving the problem of silo as I explained before everything being in one place like contract developers smart contract developers can exchange users. >> Mhm. You cannot easily do this in an L2 world, right? Even if you do it, the L2s will fight between each other and eventually for Ethereum in particular, right? And then even even on other blockchains like Solana, I don't think um they they they work under the mindset where heavier computations are coming. We we do have and we see it even on sui that people are running this uh you know payment intents where in one transaction you want to run multiple operations. Imagine if you are an AI agent or so, you need larger transactions. The the the L1 not being fast is creating a huge problem for all of these other blockchain in my opinion to go into the other step where you can do a a transaction that I buy 1% of BH Khalifa. I I sell for uh like for gold and then I buy dollars and then again I'm doing all of this cycle. But for this one, you need longer messages. You need this to be super fast using object based model because this allowed us to do these things and at the same time you need all of the cryptographic agility. I cannot see how Solana for example has this superior team on uh cryptography consensual programming language. Sui is probably the only one that actually created um starting from Facebook of course a programming language. It's is completely different as a programming language compared to even what apptos did with uh uh uh move of of Facebook right of libra >> and because I was there I built all of the cryptographic library for move and this was ported to sui and we made many changes to make it even better. So there was a reason even for many of us who left Facebook to come into mist labs and work for sui. I can give you my personal uh view here. I was the lead cryptographer in Libra like for uh for Facebook uh back then and then I had to pick between various options because at least three or four startups popped up. Everybody proposed me and for many reasons I came with some Evan and Aden and George just because it was the if you see how Libra worked it was the brains of Libra >> right I couldn't go as a researcher anywhere and you see it on the innovation pace even if other people are like proposing stuff in my opinion some of the biggest innovations are coming from us at least starting with the the initial uh like idea ZK loin is one example everybody will try to to copy it right and this This is why I believe if you want to invest into a blockchain where you cannot even predict what new feature will come in the next day, I cannot see anything else than sui and this is the reason I also joined mist labs because of this innovation rate. So I I cannot I cannot see without big big changes even on Solana they can do as much as we can for the aentic economy with multiple transactions and cryptographic agility. I think we're totally the best here. Obviously we have to do it with caution and and robustness but at the same time I cannot see any any other fast-pac uh blockchain to solve these things. >> If we if we talk about the uh agentic economy final question because uh we have a very strict time here. Um but if we talk about the agentic economy I think one of the core risk is that if you use an AI agent your AI agent get hacked gets hacked. How can we be building a security layer so we can all start using the agentic economy um in a safe manner? 100%. Let's start with the obvious thing, right? um agents are now multi-machine or multi- multi-identities because after December 2025 which in my opinion was um uh the most important uh like months for AI converting this from chat GPT basic uh texting into agents helping each other. We have many agents uh like running your trading running your whatever optimization tools you want. >> We [snorts] need we need identities and we need key management. All of these a entities even if they hacked they get hacked. We need to ensure that they someone cannot steal the uh the keys the the private keys so they can you know offiscate fake their identities. >> So in SUI we built a protocol that is called seal like the animal which is actually decentralized key management. People if you see even Adrien Horovich mentioning in one of the upcoming uh trends in crypto it was this. Do you know where they were inspired from from us? Because seal is indeed a protocol with uh where with MPC [snorts] we do have the validators of uh like three or even you can create your own your own cluster to handle secrets for agents, handle secrets for users. How can we have confidential transactions because agents also want to hide their stuff >> otherwise you can front them. You there there is an ME situation here. you need one of the best uh crypto agility on the on the key management. In my opinion, there is no other effort out there that is actually solving this particular issue but as part of the L1 along with Walrus where now the agents can go and store all of the big data there. what do you do on the other blockchains and you see some other blockchains mentioning maybe we should work with walrus as well even if it's built on on suite because there is no other alternative at the moment right even IPFS and the rest are by far older h come on and and walrus is policy based people can do whatever they want there >> yes you agent you can have access to this data you agent you can have access to other data so we can create a policy between who has access to what >> well um thank you very much for coming on the show to explain everything surrounding the recent events, but also everything that you guys are building. Um, good luck in the coming period. >> Good luck here in Paris >> and hopefully we see you again soon on the show. >> I love it by the way. Great great setup here violin. It's It's amazing. >> It's actually It's a beautiful venue. That's correct. Yeah. Thank you very much, man. >> Thank you. Thank you, Paris. Thank you.
